Q1: I tried to keep a pretty consistent study routine, honestly. I'd block out about four to five hours each day, usually in the mornings when my mind feels clearer. Being a visual learner, I'd organize my notes with color-coded sections and diagrams, which took time but helped me retain information. Even though I put in around 32 hours a week, lately everything just feels heavy, like I'm going through the motions without really feeling it.
Q2: I definitely leaned toward regular times each week rather than cramming before deadlines. I'd set specific hours in the morning and stick to them most days. That said, when multiple assignments piled up, I'd extend into evenings too. The consistency helped me maintain my attendance and keep up with the 15 online courses I was juggling, even when my motivation was running low.
Q3: Usually I'd log in first thing in the morning to check for any announcements or new materials. Sometimes it was just habit, like checking my phone when I wake up. Other times, I'd get a notification about a discussion post or a peer message that pulled me in. The peer support group I'm part of often prompted me to log in just to see what others were sharing or asking about.
Q4: Yeah, logging into the LMS became pretty automatic after the first few weeks. I think it happened because I made it part of my morning routine, right after breakfast. The discussion forums and peer interactions kept me coming back too, since I'd want to respond to classmates. It became less of a chore and more of a habit, even on days when I wasn't feeling great emotionally.
Q5: The video lectures were definitely the most useful for me. I watched over 260 minutes of them because seeing concepts visually really helps things click for me. The discussion forums were also valuable because interacting with peers gave me different perspectives. When I'm feeling down, sometimes reading what others are struggling with makes me feel less alone in this whole thing.
Q6: I'll admit I didn't complete as many tasks as I probably should have, only about 35% of them. Some of the reading-heavy resources I'd skim or delay because walls of text just don't stick for me the way visuals do. If something didn't have diagrams or videos, I'd often put it off. It's not that I thought they were useless, I just couldn't muster the energy sometimes.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate relevance to exams or assignments. Visual content like diagrams, flowcharts, or video explanations always felt more valuable to me. Also, if peers were actively discussing something, that made it feel more important. When I'm already struggling with low motivation, I need to see clear payoff to push myself to engage.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford emotionally. Required ones had a deadline and consequences, so I'd force myself through them even when I felt sad or drained. Optional ones, I'd tell myself I'd get to them later, but often that later never came. There's this guilt that comes with skipping them, but sometimes I just didn't have it in me.
Q9: Absolutely, I did a lot of studying offline that wouldn't show up in the LMS. I'd download lecture slides and PDFs, then annotate them on my tablet with different colors and diagrams. I also used YouTube for supplementary explanations when the course videos weren't clicking. A lot of my actual learning happened with printed notes spread across my desk, not logged in anywhere.
Q10: There were many times I'd be reviewing my handwritten notes or working through problems on paper for hours. The LMS would show I logged in once to download something, but it wouldn't capture the three hours I spent afterward working through that material. My visual study maps and diagrams took real effort to create, but they exist only in my notebooks, invisible to any tracking system.
Q11: Yes, this was pretty common for me. I'd access a video lecture or PDF once, download it, and then revisit it multiple times offline over the following days. The LMS would only register that single access, not the four or five times I reviewed it on my tablet during the week. It made more sense to work offline where I could annotate freely without needing internet constantly.
Q12: There were definitely periods when engaging became really hard, usually when the sadness hit harder than usual. Some weeks I'd feel this heaviness that made even logging in feel exhausting. The workload from 15 courses didn't help either. During those times, I'd still show up because of my high resilience, but I wasn't really present mentally, just surviving.
Q13: The workload definitely affected how I used the LMS. When I was overwhelmed, I'd prioritize watching videos over reading text because it required less active effort from me. Fatigue from stress made me skip discussion posts some weeks, even though I usually enjoy them. Competing responsibilities weren't huge since I don't do extracurriculars, but emotional exhaustion was a constant battle.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd be watching a video but my mind was completely elsewhere, just sad and unfocused. I'd get to the end and realize I hadn't absorbed anything, so I'd have to rewatch it later. The LMS would show I watched it, but meaningful engagement wasn't happening. It's frustrating because I was trying, but my brain just wouldn't cooperate.
Q15: I postponed some tasks even when I knew they mattered. Part of it was low motivation making everything feel like climbing a mountain. Part of it was that some activities felt less rewarding, like dense readings without visual aids. I'd tell myself I'd do them when I felt better, but that better mood didn't always come. My assignment scores stayed high because I prioritized those, but other things slipped.
Q16: Deadlines were honestly my main driver. Without them, I probably would have engaged even less given my current motivation levels. I'd organize my LMS usage around upcoming due dates, making sure to watch relevant videos and check discussions before assignments. The structure deadlines provided was helpful, but it also meant I was often reactive rather than proactive.
Q17: Feedback did affect my engagement, though sometimes in complicated ways. Good feedback on assignments motivated me to keep putting in effort despite feeling down. But when feedback was slow or generic, it reinforced this feeling that maybe my effort didn't matter much. The peer support helped fill that gap, having classmates acknowledge my contributions meant a lot.
Q18: The course structure with regular video releases and discussion prompts helped me stay on track. Having content broken into weekly chunks made it manageable alongside the other courses. The peer interaction components were particularly valuable for me because connecting with others helped combat the isolation that comes with feeling sad. Without that structure, I might have fallen behind completely.
Q19: Visual resources made engagement much easier for me since that's how I learn best. The discussion forums were well-designed and encouraged participation, which I appreciated. What made things harder was when modules were text-heavy without visual aids or when there were too many small tasks to track. My task completion rate suffered partly because the sheer number of items felt overwhelming.
Q20: I think high activity with low submissions could mean someone is struggling to convert their learning into finished work. Maybe they're anxious about submitting imperfect work, or they're using the LMS to learn but can't synthesize it into assignments. For me, I watch a lot of videos and engage in discussions, but my exam scores don't fully reflect all that engagement. The processing takes time.
Q21: That actually resonates with me somewhat. My LMS activity is high, but a lot of my actual learning happens offline with my visual notes and diagrams. Someone could download materials once and master them through offline study. The LMS might show minimal activity while the student is deeply engaged with the content in ways that aren't tracked. Performance comes from understanding, not just clicks.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show patterns like login frequency, time spent on videos, and participation in discussions. But they miss the quality of that engagement, whether someone was actually absorbing content or just going through motions. They can't capture offline study, emotional state during learning, or the depth of understanding. My 264 minutes of video watching doesn't show that some of it was rewatched because I was too sad to focus the first time.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when I'm actively connecting new concepts to what I already know, creating those visual maps in my head. It's participating in discussions because I'm genuinely curious, not just for points. Even when I'm feeling low, real engagement is when some part of me still wants to learn and grow.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much emotional energy it takes for me to engage when I'm feeling sad and unmotivated. It wouldn't capture the peer support conversations that happen outside the platform, or the hours I spend with my colored notes and diagrams. It can't measure resilience, how I keep showing up with 96.5% attendance despite everything feeling heavy. The human struggle behind the data points stays invisible.
